costs: Compensatory funding or capacity controls for general schools absorbing displaced demand — medium-to-high, multi-year, currently unbudgeted; no numeric fiscal range has been published in Hungarian sources, and fiscal consolidation pressure (deficit projected at 6.2% in 2026) further reduces the credibility of any future budget commitment.
Regulatory design and legal-drafting costs for capacity-cap or threshold-setting instruments across maintainer types, magnitude unpublished.
Administrative monitoring costs to track maintainer-level compliance and detect offsetting expansion by non-state maintainers, currently unbudgeted.
content.equity_impact: Residual selective places are likely to become more SES-skewed as competition intensifies for a smaller number of slots, and lower-SES pupils lose access without compensating investment in general-school quality; the scenario does not offer any mechanism, safeguard, or funding commitment to mitigate this risk of intensified within-remaining-places sorting, nor does it address church/foundation-sector expansion that could absorb and even increase overall selective-track access despite state-sector contraction.
expected_benefits: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
May incrementally improve peer composition in urban general-school upper grades by retaining higher-performing students who would otherwise have exited into shrunk selective capacity.
content.goal: Shrink the state-sector footprint of 6/8-year gimnázium places via score thresholds, capacity caps, or consolidation, while leaving admission criteria within remaining places largely unchanged.

implementation_steps.action: Formalize currently discretionary minimum-score thresholds into a consistent, published national capacity-reduction schedule to eliminate the current two-tier administrative chaos (binding vs. non-binding cutoffs).
implementation_steps.actor: Klebelsberg Center / state school district maintainers

implementation_steps.action: Design and propose regulatory levers (funding conditions, licensing terms) aimed at discouraging offsetting capacity expansion by church and foundation maintainers, acknowledging the coalition-constraint and legal-autonomy barriers documented in the expert record; a credible design requires legal review of existing maintainer autonomy agreements before any lever can be enacted.
Publish annual monitoring reports tracking selective-track share of cohort, SES composition of remaining places, and any offsetting non-state expansion.
Allocate compensatory capacity/quality investment to general schools absorbing displaced demand, contingent on fiscal-space availability under consolidation pressure.

mechanisms: Minimum-score admission thresholds set by maintainers (e.g., the Klebelsberg Center) reduce the gimnázium track's share of cohort intake from approximately 40% toward 30% or lower, independent of demand.

Reduced state-sector selective capacity may incrementally improve peer composition in urban general-school upper grades, though the international evidence base for this specific compositional mechanism (capacity reduction rather than track abolition) is contested and cycle-dependent per PISA equity-trend reviews.

Demographic decline compounds the score-threshold lever, since the absolute pool of eligible students is shrinking independently of policy, meaning capacity caps interact with an already-contracting applicant base.
risks: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.

The lack of a clear normative justification (efficiency vs equity vs demography) for capacity reduction independent of demand creates messaging vulnerability, since parents may perceive it as arbitrary rationing rather than principled reform.
content.title: Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)
